Glossary About
Machine Minds

John von Neumann

1903 – 1957

Computation, formal systems, and the psychophysical puzzles at the edge of machine thought.

The safest conclusion is that von Neumann did not leave a systematic “philosophy of mind” in the way that Descartes, Brentano, Husserl, Ryle, or Chalmers did. What he left is something more fragmentary but still philosophically powerful: a set of arguments about the observer in quantum mechanics, the formal realizability of nervous-system functions, the computational and statistical structure of the brain, the historical contingency of logic and mathematics, and a late-life religious/theological horizon that cannot simply be dismissed as biographical noise.

A coherent philosophy can be extracted from these materials, but only if we keep two cautions in view. First, von Neumann’s “consciousness” in quantum mechanics is not straightforwardly the same topic as his “brain” in automata theory. Second, his deathbed Catholic turn and remarks about God are relevant to “soul,” mortality, and value, but they are not a formal scientific theory of the soul.

My reconstructed thesis is this: von Neumann’s most defensible philosophy of mind is a methodological psychophysical parallelism joined to a non-naïve computational naturalism. Subjective experience is epistemically irreducible: all empirical science ultimately reports what is observed by an observer. But science must also treat subjective processes as having physical correlates. Describable mental functions are mechanically realizable in principle, yet the actual brain is not a serial digital computer; it is a massively parallel, statistical, biologically constrained natural automaton whose true “language” is not ordinary conscious mathematics. Theology, when added carefully, does not become a “God of the gaps” inside physics; rather, it interprets the subject as a moral and possibly immortal person at the limit where scientific objectification stops.

1. The basic evidence

The central sources are five clusters.

The first is Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Mechanics, especially Chapter VI on measurement. There von Neumann distinguishes two quantum processes: a causal, reversible evolution and the non-causal transition associated with measurement, where a state goes into a mixture with definite probabilities. He then introduces subjective perception and psychophysical parallelism as part of the analysis of measurement.

The second is the Hixon Symposium paper, “The General and Logical Theory of Automata,” where von Neumann compares artificial automata, neural networks, and the central nervous system. He treats natural organisms as vastly more complicated and subtle than artificial automata, while still arguing that any function describable in finite, unambiguous terms can in principle be realized by a finite formal neural network.

The third is The Computer and the Brain, his unfinished late work based on the Yale Silliman lectures he was too ill to deliver. The Institute for Advanced Study notes that he worked on these lectures while ill and that the book was published posthumously in 1958; the same IAS page quotes a 1957 Life article describing the brain treatise as the work von Neumann thought might be his “crowning achievement.”

The fourth is his broader philosophy of mathematics and logic. In “The Mathematician,” he stresses that mathematics is not simply an empirical science, yet its development remains deeply linked to the natural sciences and to empirical sources. In the 1936 Birkhoff–von Neumann paper on quantum logic, he and Birkhoff argue that quantum theory forces a non-classical propositional structure, including the breakdown of distributivity.

The fifth is biographical and theological evidence: his Catholic baptism for marriage, his generally non-practicing life, reports that colleagues saw him as agnostic, his seeking a priest and receiving last rites while dying, the reported remark that God’s existence made many things easier to explain, and Father Anselm Strittmatter’s funeral allocution with explicit language about spirit, soul, resurrection, judgment, suffering, and moral responsibility.

2. Quantum mechanics: subjective perception as irreducible, but not magical

Von Neumann’s most famous mind-related passage occurs in his discussion of measurement. He says that measurement, or the related process of subjective perception, is a “new entity” relative to the physical environment and is not reducible to it. His reason is not first a theological reason; it is epistemological. Subjective perception belongs to the inner life of the individual and is “extra-observational” because every observation or experiment already presupposes it.

But the next move is just as important. He immediately says that the scientific viewpoint requires psychophysical parallelism: the process of subjective perception must be describable “as if” it occurred in the physical world, by assigning to it equivalent physical processes in ordinary space. That is the key to von Neumann’s position. He is not saying, “mind is outside science, therefore science stops.” He is saying: experience cannot be eliminated as the condition of observation, yet science must correlate experience with objective physical processes.

His thermometer example makes this precise. We can stop the measurement chain at the thermometer, or carry it forward through reflected light, the eye, the retina, the optic nerve, and the brain. However far we carry the physical description, at some point the account must say that something is perceived by an observer. The world is therefore divided into an observed system and an observer, but the exact boundary is to a large extent arbitrary.

This arbitrariness is crucial. Von Neumann says the observer need not be identified with the body of the actual observer; the boundary can be moved into the observer’s body, even toward the brain. In one formulation, system I is what is observed, system II is the measuring instrument, and system III is the actual observer; in the most inward version, III is the observer’s abstract “ego,” which remains outside the calculation.

That “abstract ego” is philosophically tempting, but it must be handled carefully. It does not mean that von Neumann straightforwardly proposed an immaterial soul that physically collapses the wavefunction. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy explicitly warns that neither von Neumann nor Dirac made conscious awareness a necessary condition for collapse; in von Neumann, the cut may be placed between system and apparatus, or pushed into the perceptual apparatus, precisely because of psychophysical parallelism. The SEP also notes that Wigner’s later consciousness-causes-collapse interpretation involved rejecting von Neumann’s psychophysical parallelism, not simply continuing it.

Recent scholarship has pressed the same point. Laudisa argues that nothing in von Neumann’s text literally justifies the inference that subjective perception causes collapse, and he criticizes later readings that transform von Neumann’s cautious discussion into a causal power of consciousness. Laudisa also notes that von Neumann’s formal model of measurement concerns the joint system of the measured object and apparatus, relating the state of the object to properties of the apparatus such as pointer positions.

So the proper interpretation is not “consciousness creates physical reality.” It is this: quantum mechanics forces physics to confront the fact that empirical content is observer-indexed. Experience is not one more object inside the observed system, because any observed system is known only through experience. Yet the scientific attitude demands that experience be correlated with physical events. That is a subtle parallelist position, not crude idealism.

3. The 1939 Ortvay correspondence: consciousness, life, and non-spatial description

The quantum-measurement discussion was not an isolated moment. Hungarian and English materials on the von Neumann–Ortvay correspondence show that von Neumann continued thinking about the observer, the brain, and psychophysical parallelism in the late 1930s.

A published English translation/OCR of “The von Neumann–Ortvay Connection” presents a 1939 exchange in which von Neumann says he is deeply interested in biology, quantum theory, and the anatomy of the brain; he also entertains the possibility that processes inherently related to life cannot be adequately described geometrically or spatially. In the same exchange he says he has been thinking about the nature of the observer in quantum mechanics and asks for further thoughts on psychophysical parallelism.

A Hungarian Typotex edition of selected writings, introduced by Ropolyi László, similarly notes that von Neumann’s 1939 letters bring up philosophical questions and show that he was still occupied with the psychophysical parallelism treated in his 1932 quantum book, as well as with a critical view of Carnap’s philosophy of science.

This matters because it shows that von Neumann’s mind-problem was not merely “Can the brain compute?” Before the late automata work, he was already thinking about the observer, life, non-geometrical description, and the limits of spatial representation. The later automata writings do not abolish this concern; they redirect it into questions of organization, coding, formal description, neural networks, and biological complexity.

4. Automata and the brain: mechanization without naïve reductionism

Von Neumann’s automata theory looks, at first, like a hard mechanistic program. In the Hixon paper, he says natural organisms are much more complicated and less well understood than artificial automata, but also that artificial automata can illuminate natural organization and vice versa. Computing machines, he says, resemble the central nervous system or at least a segment of its functions, while being vastly less complicated.

The strongest mechanistic claim comes from his discussion of McCulloch and Pitts. He states that any functioning that can be logically, strictly, and unambiguously defined in a finite number of words can be realized by a formal neural network. He then draws the anti-vitalist consequence: if a nervous-system function is completely and unambiguously describable, then there is no principled barrier to mechanical neural realization.

But this is not simple-minded reductionism. Von Neumann immediately emphasizes two remaining difficulties. First, the network may be too large to fit within the physical limits of an organism. Second, it is not clear that every existing mode of behavior can be put completely and unambiguously into words. He later says the real problem is not merely finding any equivalent network, but finding one that fits in the actual brain, runs on the actual metabolic power supply, and can be organized by actual genetic-control facilities.

This is a profound distinction. For von Neumann, “mechanically realizable in principle” does not mean “explained in the relevant biological sense.” A monster network larger than the universe would not explain the human brain. Nor would a formal description that cannot be read in a lifetime. This anticipates a distinction still central to philosophy of mind and AI: formal computability is not the same as explanatory, biological, or cognitive adequacy.

His discussion of visual analogy makes the point even sharper. He says that for extremely complex phenomena, the simplest practical description of a capacity such as visual analogy might be the connection pattern of the visual brain itself. In such cases, the object may be its own simplest description, and logic may need to undergo something like a “pseudomorphosis” into neurology.

That is not mysticism. It is a sophisticated anti-reductionism internal to mathematical science. The brain may be mechanistic, but the right mechanism may not be capturable in our familiar logical notation. The explanatory problem is not that mind is magically unmechanical; it is that mind may be a natural organization whose compression into humanly manageable formulas requires new logical languages.

5. The Computer and the Brain: the brain is digital only at the surface

In The Computer and the Brain, von Neumann begins by saying that the nervous system is prima facie digital, because neurons generate and propagate nerve impulses that can be treated as unitary events.

But almost every important conclusion complicates that first impression. Natural componentry favors many slower organs, while artificial computers favor fewer faster organs. Therefore large natural automata such as the nervous system are likely to process many informational items simultaneously, whereas large artificial computing machines tend toward serial operation. Moreover, serial and parallel schemes are not freely interchangeable, because changing the mode of organization changes memory requirements and even the logical structure of the procedure.

He also argues that the nervous system, viewed as an automaton, must have both logical and arithmetical aspects. Yet when he asks what precision the nervous system’s arithmetical side should have, he concludes that the expectation of high decimal precision is implausible. Nervous-system quantities are often transmitted by pulse frequencies, a kind of frequency-modulated signaling.

The decisive passage is his claim that the nervous system uses an essentially statistical message system. What matters is not the exact position of discrete markers, as in ordinary digital notation, but the statistical properties of pulse trains and perhaps correlations among trains. This means the nervous system trades arithmetical precision for logical reliability.

Then comes one of the most important philosophical claims in the whole corpus: the language of the brain is not the language of mathematics. Von Neumann says human languages are historical facts rather than logical necessities, and he extends that claim to logic and mathematics themselves. They may have variants different from the forms we know; the central nervous system indicates that this is so. The brain’s own language has less logical and arithmetical depth than the explicit languages of ordinary mathematics.

He even suggests that when we consciously do mathematics, we may be using a secondary language built on a primary language actually used by the central nervous system. The outward forms of mathematics are therefore not decisive for evaluating the true mathematical or logical language of the brain.

This is decisive against a crude “brain equals von Neumann computer” reading. The brain is not, for him, a serial stored-program machine running explicit symbolic mathematics. It is a natural automaton using statistical coding, massive parallelism, redundancy, low precision, and high reliability. Its underlying language may be structurally foreign to our conscious mathematical idioms.

6. Logic and mathematics: mind cannot be understood with fixed a priori categories

Von Neumann’s philosophy of mind also depends on his philosophy of logic and mathematics. In “The Mathematician,” he insists that mathematics is not practiced like an empirical science, but its development is closely linked with natural science. He describes geometry as beginning as a natural empirical science and says mathematical ideas originate in empirics, even if their later development acquires an autonomous, aesthetic life.

He also treats mathematical rigor as historically revisable. In his discussion of intuitionism, Hilbert, and Gödel, he emphasizes that major mathematicians had proposed changing the concept of exact proof itself.

The quantum-logic paper with Birkhoff radicalizes the point. They begin by saying that quantum theory attracts attention because of the novelty of the logical notions it presupposes. They identify the central difference between classical and quantum propositional calculus as the failure of the distributive law in quantum mechanics, and they conclude that physically significant statements in quantum mechanics form a projective-geometric structure rather than the Boolean algebra characteristic of classical dynamics.

This provides a general principle for interpreting his philosophy of mind: do not assume that current explicit logic is the final form of rationality. Physics forced logic to change; biology and the brain may force logic to change again. The mind may be lawful without being transparent to familiar formal systems.

7. The soul and theology: not a distraction, but a different register

The theological material must be treated neither as proof of a finished doctrine nor as irrelevant gossip. It belongs to a different register: mortality, moral responsibility, God, judgment, spirit, and the ultimate meaning of intellect.

The Society of Catholic Scientists summarizes the mixed evidence: von Neumann was Jewish by birth, accepted Catholic baptism in 1930 in order to marry, did not practice the faith, and was regarded by some colleagues as completely agnostic. Yet while dying of cancer he sought Father Anselm Strittmatter, confessed, and received the last sacraments. The same account reports that he once told his mother that there probably had to be a God, because many things were easier to explain if God existed.

MacTutor’s biography gives the existential counterpoint. It quotes Eugene Wigner saying that when von Neumann realized he was incurably ill, his logic forced him to realize that he would cease to exist and cease to have thoughts. It also reports descriptions of panic, terror, and suffering as his mind became less dependable.

This is philosophically relevant. Von Neumann’s personal identity seems, at least in Wigner’s recollection, to have been bound intensely to the continuation of thought. Death threatened not merely bodily survival but the existence of the thinking subject. That gives the Catholic deathbed material a precise philosophical role: it offered a frame in which death need not mean the cessation of the person as spirit.

Father Strittmatter’s funeral allocution explicitly says that, for believers, death is a transition from one life to another and that in the realm of spirit it does not spell dissolution or cessation. This is not von Neumann’s own philosophical treatise, but it is the theological interpretation placed over his death by the priest who ministered to him.

The same allocution also says von Neumann had come to feel the moral problems bound up with scientific power and had gained insight into a world of values that was “as real” and even more vast than the world of the atom. It reports that verses from the Dies Irae re-echoed in his soul during his last illness, and it interprets his suffering through Catholic themes of Providence, atonement, Christ’s Passion, and the soul’s rest.

The theological interpretation, then, has three components.

First, God is not introduced as a physical variable in the measurement process. The more careful reading is abductive and existential: God may make more sense of the whole than God’s absence does.

Second, the soul is not a technical posit in von Neumann’s automata theory. It appears in the Catholic register as the spiritual person whose death is not mere dissolution.

Third, moral value is not reducible to computational efficiency or physical power. The eulogy’s “world of values” is significant because von Neumann’s scientific life was deeply entangled with technological and military power. The theological frame insists that the thinking subject is also a responsible subject.

8. Non-English reception: useful interpretations and recurring distortions

The non-English reception is valuable because it shows how differently von Neumann’s observer has been read.

The Hungarian reception is especially important because it preserves the Ortvay context. Ropolyi’s introduction to selected Hungarian writings says the 1939 letters show von Neumann still occupied with psychophysical parallelism. The same introduction proposes a broader philosophical motif in von Neumann’s work: extracting order from disorder and rationally interpreting chaotic, turbulent, faulty, crisis-ridden, or irrational natural and artificial systems. A Hungarian theological article also repeats the reported God remark in Hungarian: that God’s existence is probable and makes many things easier to explain.

The German-language context helps explain “psychophysical parallelism.” Heidelberger’s study of the mind-body problem in logical empiricism notes that psychophysical parallelism was widely accepted among psychologists and physiologists into the twentieth century as a scientifically respectable doctrine preserving the autonomy of psychology alongside physiology. He also connects the tradition to Fechner, Einstein, Bohr, and von Neumann’s 1932 phrasing of the observer/system distinction. At the popular-scientific level, a Spektrum neuroscience lexicon entry lists von Neumann under quantum logics and also under speculative consciousness/quantum interpretations, illustrating how German reception sometimes groups him with later mentalist or idealist readings that require correction.

The French reception often presents a “von Neumann–Wigner–Stapp” lineage. A French educational neuroscience site says that von Neumann’s view allowed the observer to be considered as the measuring instrument or as the human brain registering the measure, while others went further and claimed that human consciousness completes the quantum measurement. That distinction is important: the French source preserves the difference between von Neumann’s movable observer-boundary and later stronger consciousness claims.

The Spanish reception is more skeptical about reduction. José Manuel Sánchez Ron, in a SciELO article, frames The Computer and the Brain as a “reductionist dream” about understanding the brain through physics and mathematics, but he also cites von Neumann’s quantum-mechanical claim about subjective apperception as irreducible to the physical environment. He later cautions that von Neumann did not finally pronounce on these deeper mind/consciousness issues and that using him as a champion of reductionism may be partly metaphorical.

The Italian reception includes a stronger subjectivist reading. Auletta writes that von Neumann introduced consciousness to justify the transition from pure state to mixture and argues that this creates a dilemma: psychophysical parallelism seems to deny that mind acts on the physical world, yet consciousness appears to be invoked to explain what physics alone cannot. A more cautious Italian thesis summary instead emphasizes the arbitrariness of the observer/system boundary and the equivalence of the predictions regardless of where the boundary is drawn.

The Russian reception is especially revealing. One Russian article on “quantum mysticism” recounts von Neumann’s thermometer-chain argument and then extrapolates dramatically, claiming that for von Neumann the answer to the origin of the observer process is God. That claim is not supported by the primary von Neumann text, but it is important as a reception phenomenon: it shows how the “abstract ego” and psychophysical parallelism can be theologically overextended. A Russian-language article on quantum approaches to consciousness is more careful: it says von Neumann’s conclusion is that measurement predictions do not depend on whether the observer boundary is placed at the apparatus or the human brain, and it explicitly says London and Bauer went much further than von Neumann by claiming that human consciousness completes measurement.

The multilingual pattern is clear. Non-English discussions often see the same tension as English discussions: von Neumann seems to make subjectivity indispensable, but it is easy to overread that into consciousness-causes-collapse, idealism, or theology. The careful reconstruction must preserve the tension without turning it into a doctrine he did not hold.

9. The extracted philosophy of mind

A comprehensive von Neumannian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, experience is epistemically primitive. Science does not finally compare theories with “bare physical values”; it compares them with observations made by observers. This is why von Neumann says experience has the form of a subjective observation rather than a free-standing assertion that a physical quantity has a value. The mind is therefore not eliminable from the epistemology of science.

Second, subjective perception is not reducible to the physical environment in the ordinary objectifying sense. It is the condition of observation, not merely another observed item. This is the strongest anti-reductionist moment in von Neumann. But it is anti-reductionist in an epistemic and methodological sense, not necessarily a full metaphysical dualism.

Third, science must nevertheless assign physical correlates to subjective processes. This is the principle of psychophysical parallelism. The mind is not simply outside nature; it must be describable as if its parts correspond to physical processes in ordinary space.

Fourth, consciousness does not, in von Neumann’s own formalism, have to be treated as a special physical force causing collapse. The observer boundary can be placed at different points, including between system and apparatus. Later consciousness-collapse interpretations, especially Wigner’s, should not be retroactively imposed on von Neumann.

Fifth, any mental or nervous-system function that can be finitely and unambiguously specified is mechanically realizable in principle. This is von Neumann’s formal anti-vitalism. There is no principled mystery in the realization of describable functions by neural networks.

Sixth, “realizable in principle” is not the same as “explained.” A functionally equivalent network may be too large, too metabolically expensive, genetically unconstructible, or expressible only in a form far more complex than the brain itself. This is von Neumann’s deep anti-naïveté about mechanistic explanation.

Seventh, the actual brain is a natural automaton whose operations are statistical, redundant, massively parallel, low-precision, and high-reliability. Its logic is not the logic of a serial digital computer. Its “language” may differ sharply from the languages of conscious mathematics.

Eighth, conscious explicit thought is probably a secondary language. When humans talk mathematics, they may be using an outward symbolic form built on a more primitive neural language. That implies a layered model of mind: neural coding at the base, explicit symbolic thought at the surface, and subjective observation as the epistemic pole of experience.

Ninth, logic itself is not fixed once and for all. Quantum mechanics required a new logical calculus; brain science may require another transformation of logic into neurology. A von Neumannian philosophy of mind should therefore be logically pluralist and scientifically fallibilist.

Tenth, the soul is not a scientific mechanism in von Neumann’s technical work. It is better understood, if integrated at all, as the theological name for the non-objectifiable, morally responsible, and possibly immortal subject. In scientific language, the nearest analogue is the “abstract ego” that remains outside the calculation; in Catholic language, it is the soul whose death is not dissolution. The two should not be collapsed into each other, but neither should their resonance be ignored.

10. What von Neumann’s view is not

It is not simple materialism. Subjective perception is treated as extra-observational and irreducible to the physical environment in the ordinary sense.

It is not Cartesian interactionism. Von Neumann does not clearly posit an immaterial mind pushing physical systems around, and the better readings of his quantum mechanics deny that he required conscious awareness as a condition for collapse.

It is not naïve computationalism. He does not say the brain is just a standard digital computer. He emphasizes parallelism, statistical notation, redundancy, low precision, different logical structure, and a primary neural language unlike explicit mathematics.

It is not mysticism. He rejects the idea that describable nervous-system functions are in principle mechanically impossible.

It is not a finished theology of mind. The Catholic deathbed materials are meaningful, but they do not amount to a technical doctrine of soul, cognition, and immortality written by von Neumann himself. They show the existential and theological frame into which his death was received.

11. The most precise label

The most precise label for the reconstructed view is:

psychophysical-parallelist computational naturalism, constrained by logical pluralism and open to theological interpretation at the level of personhood, value, and death.

“Psychophysical-parallelist” means subjective experience is epistemically irreducible but must have physical correlates.

“Computational naturalism” means describable cognitive functions are realizable by natural or artificial automata.

“Constrained” means the brain’s actual organization may not be capturable by familiar serial, symbolic, high-precision computation.

“Logical pluralism” means the forms of logic and mathematics are not timelessly fixed; physical and biological reality can force new formal languages.

“Open to theology” means God and soul are not variables inside his physics, but his late-life religious turn and the Catholic interpretation of his death are relevant to the ultimate status of the subject, moral responsibility, and the meaning of mortality.

12. Final synthesis

Von Neumann’s philosophy of mind, if reconstructed responsibly, has a remarkable architecture.

At the bottom is the brain as natural automaton: physical, finite, organized, statistical, noisy, redundant, massively parallel.

Above that are cognitive functions: insofar as they can be unambiguously described, they are mechanically realizable.

Above that are explicit languages: ordinary language, mathematics, and logic, which are historically developed symbolic systems and may not match the brain’s primary code.

At the epistemic limit is subjective observation: the unavoidable standpoint from which empirical science has meaning.

At the theological limit is the soul: not a scientific explanatory device, but the possible name for the enduring, morally accountable subject whose death is not merely the failure of a machine.

That is the most coherent philosophy of mind one can extract from von Neumann without distorting him. It is neither reductionist nor anti-scientific, neither mystical nor eliminative. Its central insight is that mind must be correlated with the physical world, functionally modeled where possible, formally re-described with new logics where necessary, and existentially interpreted where science reaches the boundary of observation, value, and death.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
